  • 1st year apprentice: approximately $22/h
  • 4th year apprentice: approximately $36/h
  • Journeyman plumber: $45 to $52/h
  • Master plumber (CMMTQ + RBQ): $55 to $75/h
  • Maintenance plumber (annual salary): $55,000 to $75,000
  • Construction plumber (before bonuses): $85,000 to $105,000/year

question: What are the requirements for fire sprinklers in Quebec?
answer:
Fire sprinkler systems in Quebec are governed by NFPA 13 (Standard for the Installation of Sprinkler Systems) and the Quebec Construction Code:

Requirements:

  • Residential buildings of 4 stories or more: sprinklers required in all units and common areas
  • Commercial/industrial buildings depending on area and use (warehouses, factories, commercial spaces over 600 m²)
  • Healthcare facilities, daycare centres, seniors' residences: required

Types of systems:

  • Wet pipe system: Most common — water under pressure in pipes at all times. Response time: 30-60 seconds
  • Dry pipe system: For unheated areas (parking, warehouse). Pipes under air pressure, water arrives after activation
  • Pre-action system: For computer rooms — requires double trigger (detection + heat)

Installation requirements:

  • Pipes in Schedule 40 steel or CPVC for wet systems
  • Sprinkler spacing: maximum 4.6 meters (15 feet) between heads
  • Coverage per head: up to 12.1 m² (130 ft²) for standard heads

Installation must be done by a CMMTQ-certified contractor with the appropriate RBQ license.

question: What are the gas standards in Quebec (CSA B149)?
answer:
In Quebec, gas installations are governed by the CSA B149.1 standard (Natural Gas and Propane Installation Code):

Key points of the standard:

  • Gas installation must be done by a CMMTQ-certified plumber specifically qualified in gas
  • Pipes: Schedule 40 steel (threaded) or CSST (corrugated stainless steel tubing). CSST costs about $2/ft compared to $5/ft for threaded steel
  • CSST must be bonded with a #6 AWG copper conductor to prevent lightning puncture
  • Pressure: 7 to 11 inches of water column (WC) for residential natural gas

Purging and testing:

  • Pressure test: 15 PSI for at least 15 minutes for new installations
  • Purging (removing air from pipes) must be done outdoors, never indoors
  • A carbon monoxide (CO) detector is mandatory in any room containing a gas appliance (boiler, water heater, range)

question: What drain size is required for a shower or bathtub?
answer:
Fixture drain sizes are defined in the Quebec Plumbing Code:

Bathtub:

  • Drain diameter: 1 ½ in (38 mm) minimum
  • The trap must be a 1 ½ in P-trap, accessible via an access panel
  • The overflow: connected to the drain downstream of the trap

Shower:

  • Drain diameter: 2 in (51 mm) minimum (larger than bathtub to drain large water volume quickly)
  • The shower base must have a slope of at least ¼ in per foot (2%) toward the drain
  • Maximum distance from trap to vertical drain: 1.5 meters
  • A shower drain must have a vent stack (revent) within 1.2 meters

Typical flow rates:

  • Bathtub: 10-15 GPM when filling
  • Shower: 2-5 GPM depending on the showerhead (WaterSense heads limit to 2 GPM)

A walk-in shower (zero-entry) requires a linear drain or a specifically designed base and an accessible trap.

question: How to get an RBQ license as a plumber?
answer:
To obtain an RBQ plumber contractor license, you must follow these steps:

Steps:

  1. CMMTQ Certification: Hold a certificate of competence from the CMMTQ (passed exam + completed 8,000 hours of apprenticeship)
  2. CCQ Card: Be registered with the CCQ with a plumbing mechanic qualification
  3. Experience: Have 2 to 4 years of experience as a journeyman (depending on the license subcategory)
  4. RBQ Application: Submit an online application via the RBQ portal, with the following documents: CMMTQ certificate, CCQ card, proof of experience, insurance (liability: $2,000,000 minimum)
  5. RBQ Exam: Pass the RBQ administration exam (laws, regulations, management)

License categories:

  • Specialized subcontractor (RBQ 2.1): For small jobs, fee $150/year
  • General contractor (RBQ 1.3): For all plumbing work, fee $300 to $800/year

question: How to install a washing machine drain in Quebec?
answer:
Washing machine drain installation must comply with the Quebec Plumbing Code:

Water drainage:

  • Drain pipe: 2 in (51 mm) minimum diameter
  • Drain loop height: the washing machine drain hose must be at least 600 mm (24 in) from the floor and at most 900 mm (36 in)
  • The washing machine hose must insert into the standpipe at least 100 mm (4 in) — never sealed
  • A trap is required on the standpipe, at least 150 mm (6 in) below the washing machine hose entry

Water supply:

  • Shut-off valves: ½ in, with braided flexible hoses
  • Washing machine hoses should be reinforced rubber or braided stainless steel (more durable)

Hot water: A washing machine uses about 30-40 L of water per load. The recommended hot water temperature is 50°C to 60°C for effective cleaning without damaging fabrics.

question: How to install a water softener in Quebec?
answer:
Installing a water softener in Quebec follows specific rules to protect the potable water network:

Location:

  • Install the softener after the water meter and after the expansion tank (if present)
  • Often installed in the basement, near the main water entrance, in a drained area
  • A bypass valve is mandatory to allow isolating the softener without shutting off water to the whole house

Connection:

  • Inlet/outlet: 1 in for standard houses with PEX or copper piping
  • Regeneration drain: ½ in pipe directed to a floor drain or standpipe, with an air gap of at least 20 mm
  • Prohibition: The regeneration drain must never be directly connected to a sewer pipe (contamination risk)

Benefits:

  • Reduces scale in pipes and appliances
  • Extends water heater lifespan by 30-50%
  • Reduces soap/detergent consumption by 50%

Installation must be done by a CMMTQ-certified plumber. The complete cost (unit + installation) ranges from $1,500 to $3,500.

question: Electric or gas boiler: how to choose?
answer:
The choice between an electric and gas boiler for water heating in Quebec depends on several factors:

Electric boiler:

  • Efficiency: 95-99% (nearly all electricity becomes heat)
  • Unit cost: $2,000 to $5,000
  • Installation: Simple, no chimney, no gas to manage
  • Lifespan: 15-20 years
  • Operating cost: Higher — electricity in Quebec costs $0.073/kWh (Rate D)

Gas boiler:

  • Efficiency: 80-85% (standard) or 90-95% (high efficiency/condensing)
  • Unit cost: $3,000 to $8,000
  • Installation: More complex — gas venting (concentric or chimney), gas connection
  • Lifespan: 10-15 years
  • Operating cost: Generally 15-30% cheaper than electric

Deciding factors: If natural gas is already available on your street, connection costs about $500 to $1,500. High-efficiency (condensing) boilers are eligible for Rénoclimat program subsidies (up to $500).

question: Why does CSST need to be bonded?
answer:
CSST (Corrugated Stainless Steel Tubing) is a flexible pipe used for indoor gas installations. Following several major incidents, the Quebec Construction Code requires it to be grounded (bonded):

Why: CSST has a thin wall that can be punctured by a direct or indirect lightning strike. The electrical arc passes through the gas pipe, creating a hole that causes a gas leak and potentially an explosion. Several fires in Quebec and Canada have been attributed to this phenomenon.

Requirements:

  • A #6 AWG copper bonding conductor (Section 10-206 of the Code) must connect the CSST to the panel ground bar
  • The conductor can be insulated or bare (bare is often preferred in plumbing)
  • The path should be as direct as possible — no unnecessary loops
  • The bonding connection must be installed downstream of the gas meter, between the meter and the first appliance

Cost: CSST bonding installation by a qualified plumber costs about $150 to $300. An annual inspection is recommended.
keywords: mise à la terre gaz Québec

question: Is a floor drain required in a basement in Quebec?
answer:
According to the Quebec Construction Code, a floor drain in a basement is not systematically mandatory in a residence, but it is strongly recommended:

Requirements:

  • Buildings with a water heater or boiler in the basement: a floor drain is strongly recommended, and may be required by insurers
  • Buildings with a sprinkler system: floor drain required
  • Commercial/industrial spaces: often mandatory depending on use
  • Finished basement with bathroom or laundry room: floor drain required in the laundry area

Floor drain characteristics:

  • Diameter: 4 in (102 mm) for standard floor drains
  • Trap: Must have an accessible P-trap. The trap can dry out over time — plan a water supply tap nearby
  • Grate: Steel or cast iron, must be flush with the finished concrete surface

Alternative: A floor drain with built-in check valve prevents sewer backups during heavy rain. Cost: $75 to $150 for the drain itself.

question: What is the standard kitchen sink drain configuration?
answer:
The kitchen sink drain in Quebec must comply with Plumbing Code rules:

Configuration:

  • Drain diameter: 1 ½ in (38 mm) minimum for a standard sink
  • A double basin sink: both drains connect via a T-fitting, with a single P-trap downstream
  • Maximum distance between the trap and the drain T: 300 mm (12 in)
  • Pipe bends: no tight 90° elbows — use two 45° elbows or a sweep elbow

Accessories:

  • Garbage disposal: The disposal connects directly to the sink drain (main basin side). Disposal outlet: standard 1 ½ in
  • Dishwasher: The dishwasher drain connects to the sink drain before the trap, via a rubber fitting. A high loop is required — hose secured under the counter at least 750 mm from the floor

Materials: Sink drain pipes are typically black ABS (most common in Quebec), white PVC, or stainless steel for designer sinks. ABS is preferred for its durability and ease of installation.

question: How to install an outdoor faucet (sillcock) without freezing risk?
answer:
In Quebec, an outdoor faucet (sillcock) must be installed following strict anti-freeze rules:

Golden rule: Use a frost-free sillcock. This faucet has a long body that places the shut-off valve at least 300 mm (12 in) inside the wall, in a heated space. When you close the faucet, water drains from the long tube to the outside, preventing freezing.

Installation:

  • Connection: ½ in (PEX or copper), with an accessible indoor shut-off valve
  • Slope: The frost-free faucet must be installed with a slight slope toward the outside (¼ in per foot minimum) so that water drains completely
  • Height from ground: The faucet must be at least 150 mm (6 in) above finished grade

Other winter precautions:

  • Disconnect the garden hose before the first freeze
  • Close the indoor shut-off valve and open the sillcock to drain residual water

Chrome brass outdoor sillcocks cost $30 to $60. A CMMTQ-certified plumber can install a frost-free sillcock for about $150 to $250.

question: What are the rules for a septic system in Quebec?
answer:
In Quebec, septic installations are governed by the Regulation on wastewater disposal for isolated residences (Q-2, r.22) of the MDDELCC:

Requirements:

  • A soil study (percolation test) is mandatory before any installation. Study cost: $500 to $1,200
  • The septic tank must have a minimum capacity of 2,000 litres for a 3-bedroom house, 2,800 L for 4 bedrooms
  • Minimum distance from the house: 3 meters
  • Minimum distance from a well: 15 meters (varies based on soil permeability)
  • Minimum distance from property line (ditch, lake): 15 meters

Types of systems:

  • Leach field (conventional): Perforated pipes in the ground, for permeable soil. Cost: $5,000 to $12,000
  • System with polisher (Biofilter): For low-permeability soil. Cost: $12,000 to $25,000

Installation must be done by a CMMTQ-certified contractor with the appropriate RBQ license. Tank pumping is recommended every 2 to 4 years.

question: What to check during a plumbing inspection before buying a house?
answer:
A plumbing inspection before buying a house in Quebec should cover these essential points:

What a plumbing inspector checks:

  • Water entrance: Pressure (should be between 40-60 PSI), pipe type, presence of backflow preventer
  • Water heater: Age (10-15 year lifespan), T&P valve, expansion tank, leaks, sediment
  • Visible piping: Copper (check for green pitting), PEX (check for leaks at fittings), galvanized (internal rust)
  • Toilet: Stability, base leak (wax ring), flushing, age
  • Sinks/lavatories: Flow, pressure, drains (speed), leaks under cabinet
  • Venting: Presence of revent for each fixture, roof vent unobstructed
  • Septic system (if applicable): Date of last pumping, inspection of treatment component

Red flags:

  • Galvanized pipes (lifespan exceeded — 40-50 years)
  • Rust or mold stains under sinks
  • Sewer odors (inadequate venting)
  • Fluctuating water pressure

The cost of a plumbing inspection by a CMMTQ professional is about $200 to $400.

question: How to flush and maintain an electric water heater?
answer:
Regular maintenance of an electric water heater extends its lifespan by 3 to 5 years. Here are the steps recommended by the CMMTQ:

Annual water heater flush:

  1. Turn off the power at the breaker
  2. Close the cold water supply
  3. Open the drain valve at the bottom of the water heater (connect a hose to a floor drain)
  4. Open the T&P valve or a hot water faucet upstairs to let air in
  5. Let the water drain until it runs clear (about 5-10 litres of sediment)
  6. Close the drain valve, close the T&P valve, open the cold water supply
  7. Restore power when the tank is full

Other maintenance:

  • Sacrificial anode: Check every 2-3 years. A worn anode (50% diameter reduction) must be replaced. Anode cost: $30 to $60
  • T&P Valve: Manually operate once a year to verify it's not stuck
  • Thermostat setting: 60°C (140°F) recommended — hot enough to kill bacteria (Legionella), not hot enough to cause severe burns

question: Who is responsible for water meter replacement?
answer:
Responsibility for the water meter in Quebec depends on who owns it:

Municipal water meter:

  • In most cities (Montreal, Laval, Quebec City, etc.), the meter is the property of the municipality
  • The municipality is responsible for its installation, maintenance, and replacement at no cost to the owner
  • You must provide access to the meter for reading (sent by the city 2 times a year typically)
  • If the meter is indoors and you refuse access, the city may estimate your consumption at 120% of the average

Private water meter:

  • If you have installed a sub-meter (for a rental unit or secondary dwelling), it is your property
  • Its maintenance and replacement are at your expense

Installation by a plumber:

  • Only a CMMTQ-certified plumber can install or modify piping around the meter
  • A bypass is generally required to allow maintenance without shutting off water

question: How to prevent rodents from entering through plumbing pipes?
answer:
Rodents (rats, mice) can enter a house through openings around plumbing pipes. Here's how to block them:

Typical entry points:

  • Around pipes passing through the foundation (water entrance, gas, drain)
  • Around plumbing vents on the roof
  • Space between the drain pipe and the wall under the sink

Solutions:

  • Steel wool: The best barrier — rodents cannot chew through it. Pack extra-fine (#00) steel wool into gaps around pipes. Cost: $5 per roll
  • Spray foam: Apply polyurethane foam over the steel wool to seal. Caution: some rodents chew through foam alone — always combine with steel wool
  • Metal mesh: For roof vents, install galvanized steel mesh with openings smaller than 6 mm (¼ in)
  • Metal escutcheon: Around large diameter pipes (1 in+ water entrance), a metal escutcheon screwed to the wall blocks passage

Reminder: The Plumbing Code requires that openings around pipes be sealed with non-combustible material in fire-rated walls.

question: How to connect a gas range to natural gas or propane?
answer:
Connecting a gas range must comply with the CSA B149.1 standard:

Connection:

  • The connection is made via a CSA-certified flexible stainless steel hose, maximum length 1.5 meters (5 feet)
  • The hose must be visually inspected at each installation — replace if there are cracks, deformations, or if the expiry date has passed (typically 5 years)
  • A shut-off valve is mandatory on the line, within 1.8 meters of the range, accessible at all times
  • Threaded connection: use yellow Teflon tape (gas-rated) for threads — white (plumbing) is not approved for gas

Natural gas vs propane:

  • The range must be converted if switching from natural gas to propane or vice versa. The conversion kit costs about $30 to $60
  • Natural gas pressure: 7 in WC (water column)
  • Propane pressure: 11 in WC

Important: Only a CMMTQ-certified plumber qualified in gas can perform this connection. A leak detection test is mandatory after every service (soap solution or electronic detector).

question: Why is there a sewer smell in my house?
answer:
Sewer odors in a house are caused by a problem in the plumbing system. Here are the most common causes:

Causes and solutions:

  • Dried-out trap: The trap of a floor drain, sink, or shower that hasn't been used for a while has emptied. Solution: pour 1 litre of water into each unused drain. Add 30 ml of vegetable oil to slow evaporation (oil floats on water)
  • Blocked vent: Leaves, bird nests, or ice are blocking the roof vent. Solution: inspect the roof, clear the vent. Plumber cost: $100 to $250
  • Broken toilet seal: The wax ring under the toilet is leaking. Solution: replace the wax ring ($5 to $15). If the odor persists, the toilet may be cracked
  • Broken or clogged sewer pipe: A partial clog creates pressure that forces gases through traps. Solution: drain cleaning and camera inspection ($200 to $400)

Warning: Persistent sewer odors may indicate a sewer gas (methane) problem. Open windows and contact a CMMTQ-certified plumber promptly for diagnosis.

question: What is the maximum flow rate of a showerhead in Quebec?
answer:
In Quebec, showerheads are regulated by water efficiency standards:

Maximum flow rate:

  • 2.5 GPM (9.5 L/min) at 80 PSI — legal maximum per CSA B125.1 standard
  • WaterSense (US) or Water Efficient certified showerheads: 2.0 GPM (7.6 L/min) or less
  • Some municipalities (like Montreal) require low-flow showerheads during renovations

Potential savings:

  • Switching from 2.5 GPM to 1.5 GPM saves about 10,000 to 15,000 litres of water per year (for a family of 4)
  • Water bill savings: $50 to $100/year
  • Water heating savings: $30 to $60/year (less hot water used)

Installation: Showerhead replacement — simple and doable without a plumber. Unscrew the old head (use a wrench if stuck with Teflon tape). Install the new head with thread seal tape. Time: 15 minutes. Cost of a WaterSense showerhead: $20 to $60.

question: When to install a sump pump in the basement?
answer:
A sump pump is essential in basements prone to water infiltration in Quebec.

When to install:

  • Basement below the water table level
  • History of infiltration after rain or snowmelt
  • House with a French drain that leads to a sump pit
  • High rainfall area or clay soil (poor percolation)

Types of pumps:

  • Submersible pump: Installed at the bottom of the pit, quiet, powerful. Capacity: 1/3 HP to 1 HP, flow: 2,000 to 5,000 GPH. Cost: $150 to $400
  • Pedestal pump: Motor above the pit, louder, cheaper ($100 to $200)

Installation:

  • The pit must be at least 450 mm (18 in) deep and 300 mm (12 in) in diameter
  • Discharge pipe: 1 ½ in to the outside, at least 3 meters from the foundation
  • A check valve is mandatory on the discharge pipe
  • Install a backup battery (12 V) — power outages often happen during storms. Battery cost: $100 to $250

question: How to repair a water leak under the sink?
answer:
A leak under the sink can have several causes. Here's how to diagnose and repair them:

Types of leaks:

  1. Loose trap connection: Tighten the slip nut by hand or with an adjustable wrench. If the rubber gasket is worn, replace it ($2 to $5 at the hardware store)
  2. Leaking supply hose (flexible): Rubber hoses last about 5 to 7 years. Replace with a braided stainless steel hose ($10 to $15) — more durable
  3. Faucet leaking at the base: The O-ring or cartridge is worn. Cartridge replacement: $10 to $30 depending on brand
  4. Poorly adjusted drain: The slip joint (trap adapter) has moved — tighten the nut. If the pipe is PVC/ABS, check it isn't cracked

Temporary repair (while waiting for the plumber):

  • Self-fusing silicone tape: Stretches and fuses onto itself to seal a crack. Effective for 24-48 hours
  • Pipe clamp: For a crack on a copper pipe, a clamp with a rubber sleeve can temporarily stop the leak

When to call a plumber:

  • Leak in the wall (inside the wall or in the ceiling below)
  • Punctured copper pipe (requires soldering or compression fitting)
  • Persistent leak despite repairs

question: What are the main welding processes and their differences?
answer:
The four main welding processes used in Quebec for QBQ exams are:

SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding) — Manual arc welding with covered electrode:

  • Advantages: Versatile, works on all steels, ideal for fieldwork and repairs
  • Disadvantages: Slag to remove, slow speed, less productive
  • Current: DC+ (reverse polarity) for most electrodes

GMAW (Gas Metal Arc Welding) — MIG/MAG welding:

  • Advantages: Fast, clean, ideal for production
  • Shielding gas: Argon + CO₂ (75%/25%) for steel, 100% CO₂ for economical use
  • Type: Short-circuit transfer (short arc) or spray transfer (spray arc)

FCAW (Flux Cored Arc Welding):

  • Advantages: Deep penetration, ideal for thick steel, wind resistant (no external gas for self-shielded wire)
  • Use: Heavy construction, structural frames, mining equipment

GTAW (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding) — TIG welding:

  • Advantages: Precision, aesthetic quality, welding of non-ferrous metals (aluminum, stainless, magnesium)
  • Disadvantages: Slow, requires high dexterity

question: What do the E6010, E6011, E7018 electrode codes mean?
answer:
SMAW welding electrodes are coded according to CSA W48 (identical to AWS A5.1). Let's take E7018 as an example:

Code meaning:

  • E: Electrode
  • 70: Minimum tensile strength: 70,000 PSI (490 MPa)
  • 1: Welding position — 1 = all positions, 2 = flat/horizontal only
  • 8: Coating type and polarity — basic, low hydrogen, DC+/AC

Common electrodes:

  • E6010: 60,000 PSI, all positions, cellulosic sodium coating, DC+ only. Deep penetration. Ideal for pipe and roots. Cost: about $3/kg
  • E6011: 60,000 PSI, all positions, cellulosic potassium coating, DC+/AC. Similar to E6010 but works on AC
  • E7018: 70,000 PSI, all positions, basic low hydrogen coating, DC+/AC. Most used in structural and frames. Requires drying at 300-350°C for 1 hour before use if packaging has been opened. Cost: $4 to $6/kg
  • E7024: 70,000 PSI, flat position only, rutile (iron powder) coating, high deposition. Very productive for flat fillet welds

question: What are the welding positions from 1G to 6G?
answer:
Welding positions are defined by CSA W59 and ASME Section IX. Each position tests a different skill of the welder:

For sheet metal and plates:

  • 1G (Flat): The piece is horizontal, welding from above. Easiest position, maximum deposition
  • 2G (Horizontal): Welding on a vertical joint, in the horizontal axis
  • 3G (Vertical): Welding on a vertical joint, bottom to top (uphill) or top to bottom (downhill)
  • 4G (Overhead): Welding overhead, the most difficult position

For pipe:

  • 1G: Horizontal pipe that rotates (continuous rotation) — flat welding
  • 2G: Vertical fixed pipe — horizontal welding
  • 5G: Horizontal fixed pipe — the welder goes around the pipe, combining horizontal, vertical, and overhead positions
  • 6G: Pipe inclined at 45°, fixed — the most demanding position, combining all positions. 6G certification is the most versatile and most valued by employers

At the QBQ exam, welders are typically tested in 3G and 4G positions for plates, and 6G for pipe. A 6G-certified welder's salary is 15-25% higher than a welder certified only in flat position.

question: What CSA standards apply to welding in Quebec?
answer:
Several CSA (Canadian Standards Association) standards govern welding in Quebec. The main ones for a QBQ welder are:

CSA W47.1 — Certification of welding companies:

  • Requires welding companies to have an approved quality control program
  • Classes: Division 1 (all processes, engineer required) and Division 2 (SMAW/FCAW only, limited procedures)
  • A certified welding supervisor must oversee operations

CSA W59 — Welding of steel structures (carbon steel):

  • The Canadian equivalent of AWS D1.1, with Canada/Quebec-specific amendments
  • Defines weld acceptance criteria, WPS (Pre-qualified Welding Procedures), tolerances
  • Requires welders to be certified per CSA W47.1 by a body such as QBQ

CSA W178 — Qualification of inspection personnel:

  • Certification of welding inspectors (visual inspectors, CGSB) and testing laboratories
  • Inspector levels: Level 1 (assistant), Level 2 (independent), Level 3 (expert)

question: How long is the QBQ certification valid and how does the exam work?
answer:
The QBQ (Quebec Bureau of Welder Qualification) certification is valid for 2 years. Here are the exam and renewal details:

Practical exam:

  • The candidate welds one or more test plates in the required positions (typically 3G and/or 4G)
  • The plates are then laboratory tested: bend test, tensile test, and macro-examination
  • Practical exam duration: 1 to 3 hours depending on the number of plates
  • Cost: about $150 to $300 including destructive tests

For pipe:

  • Exam in 6G position (45° inclined pipe): welding a pipe of 4 in to 6 in diameter, 8 mm minimum wall thickness
  • Tests include bending, radiography (RT), or tensile testing

Renewal:

  • Valid for 2 years
  • To renew: proof of continuous welding work (minimum 6 months out of 2 years) and passing a verification test (retest)
  • If the certification has been expired for more than 6 months, the full exam must be retaken

question: What personal protective equipment (PPE) is mandatory for welding?
answer:
Welding safety is governed by the CNESST in Quebec. Mandatory PPE includes:

Eye and face protection:

  • Welding helmet with shade filter: #9 to #14 depending on the process and intensity. For SMAW with E7018 (130 A), shade #10 or #11 recommended
  • Safety glasses under the helmet (with side shields)
  • Auto-darkening helmets are allowed: transition time 1/10,000 to 1/25,000 second

Respiratory protection:

  • Welding fumes contain toxic metals (manganese, hexavalent chromium, iron oxides)
  • Helmet with integrated air filtration system (PAPR): highly recommended for confined space welding
  • Wear an N95 or N99 respirator for shop work

Body protection:

  • Welding jacket in flame-resistant leather (no cotton or synthetic that can burn)
  • Welding gloves: thick leather (size 9-12), long cuffs protecting the wrists
  • Leather apron for heavy work
  • Safety boots with steel toe (CSA Z195)
  • Hearing protection: earplugs or earmuffs (grinding exceeds 85 dB)

question: What shielding gases to use for MIG/MAG and TIG welding?
answer:
The choice of shielding gas is crucial for weld quality. Here are the gases used for each process:

MIG/MAG (GMAW) welding on carbon steel:

  • 75% Argon + 25% CO₂ — The standard mix for steel (C25). Controlled penetration, good arc stability, little spatter. Flow: 12-15 L/min
  • 100% CO₂ — Cheaper (50% of the price of C25 mix), deeper penetration, but more spatter and a rougher arc
  • 98% Argon + 2% O₂ — For very stable spray arc transfer

MIG welding on aluminum:

  • 100% Argon — Mandatory. Flow: 15-20 L/min

TIG (GTAW) welding:

  • Steel/stainless: 100% Argon. Flow: 8-12 L/min
  • Aluminum: 100% Argon or 80% Argon + 20% Helium (helium increases heat input for thick pieces)
  • Copper and alloys: 100% Helium (hotter than argon for conducting heat)

Gas costs (medium cylinder ~200 ft³):

  • Argon: $80 to $120
  • C25 mix: $60 to $90
  • 100% CO₂: $40 to $60

question: What is a welder's salary in Quebec in 2025?
answer:
A welder's salary in Quebec varies by certification, experience, and employment sector:

Average hourly wage:

  • Entry-level welder (recent QBQ certification): $25 to $30/h
  • Intermediate welder (3-5 years experience): $32 to $40/h
  • Advanced welder (5-10 years, 6G/6GR certified): $40 to $55/h
  • Specialized welder (TIG, stainless, aluminum, shipyard): $50 to $70/h
  • Pipeline welder (travelling): $60 to $100/h (with travel premiums)

Annual salary:

  • Shop work (40 hrs/week): $50,000 to $85,000
  • Construction site (CCQ): $75,000 to $110,000
  • With overtime: $100,000 to $150,000 (pipeline, major projects)

Highest-paying sectors in Quebec: shipbuilding (Davie, Lévis Shipyard), aluminum smelters (Alma, Jonquière), mines (Abitibi, North Shore), pipelines (Gazoduq, Energy East). Remote location premiums can add 15-30% to the base salary.

question: How to become a welder in Quebec?
answer:
To become a certified welder in Quebec, here is the typical pathway:

Training pathway:

  1. DEP in welding: Diploma of Vocational Studies in welding and fitting, offered at several centres like CFP de Québec, CFP Léonard-de-Vinci, or CFP de la Baie-James. Duration: 1,350 hours (about 15 months). Cost: $500 to $1,000 for tuition fees
  2. ASP (Attestation of Professional Specialization): In advanced welding (TIG, special steels) — 600 to 900 additional hours
  3. QBQ Certification: Practical welding exam with the Quebec Bureau of Welder Qualification

Skills learned in DEP:

  • SMAW (covered electrode), GMAW (MIG), FCAW (flux-cored), GTAW (TIG)
  • Blueprint reading and welding symbols
  • Basic metallurgy, safety, CSA W59/W47.1 standards

After the DEP:

  • QBQ certification is often required by employers (minimum 3G and 4G)
  • Some employers offer sponsorship for the QBQ exam (cost covered by the company)
  • A welder can specialize in pipe (6G certification), stainless steel, or aluminum

question: What is the HAZ and why is preheating important?
answer:
The HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) is the region of the base metal that has undergone metallurgical changes due to welding heat, without being melted.

Characteristics of the HAZ:

  • Typical width: 2 to 5 mm on each side of the weld
  • Modified grain structure: coarse grains near the fusion line, fine grains further away
  • The most brittle zone of the weld — risk of cracking if cooling is too fast

Why preheating:

  • Slows down the cooling of the HAZ, allowing martensitic (hard) transformation into more ductile structures
  • Reduces HAZ hardness (recommended limit: 350 HV max for carbon steels)
  • Reduces the risk of underbead cracking

Recommended preheat temperatures:

  • Mild steel (< 0.25% C): 50-100°C if thickness > 25 mm
  • Medium carbon steel (0.25-0.50% C): 100-200°C
  • High-strength steel: 150-300°C

Preheat temperature is measured with a surface thermometer or temperature-indicating crayon, at 75 mm from the joint.

question: What non-destructive testing (NDT) methods are used in welding?
answer:
Non-destructive testing (NDT) methods allow checking weld quality without destroying the part. The most common methods:

1. Visual inspection (VT)

  • The first step of any inspection. Checks: weld appearance, porosity, visible cracks, arc strikes, craters, undercut, overlap
  • The inspector must be certified CGSB Level 1 or 2

2. Radiography (RT) — X-ray or Gamma

  • Radiographic image of the weld revealing internal defects: slag inclusions, lack of fusion, internal porosity, cracks
  • Standard: CSA W59 or ASME Section V
  • Cost: $100 to $300 per film

3. Ultrasonics (UT)

  • High-frequency sound waves (2-10 MHz) that detect planar defects (cracks, lack of fusion) that radiography may miss
  • Requires a CGSB Level 2 certified operator
  • Faster and cheaper than radiography ($50 to $150 per hour)

4. Dye penetrant (PT) and Magnetic particle (MT)

  • Dye penetrant: For detecting surface cracks on any non-porous material
  • Magnetic particle: For magnetic materials (steel). Magnetic particles reveal surface and subsurface defects

question: What is the difference between uphill and downhill vertical welding?
answer:
Vertical welding can be done in two ways, each with its advantages:

Downhill vertical welding:

  • Direction: top to bottom
  • Technique: The electrode or torch is tilted upward (push angle of 5-15°)
  • Advantages: Fast (2-3 times faster than uphill), less metal deposition, less penetration
  • Ideal for: Thin sheets (< 6 mm), thin-wall pipe (pipeline), root passes
  • Often uses E6010 electrodes for fast penetration

Uphill vertical welding:

  • Direction: bottom to top
  • Technique: The electrode or torch is tilted downward (drag angle of 5-15°)
  • Advantages: Deeper penetration, better sidewall fusion, thicker metal deposition
  • Ideal for: Thick plates (> 6 mm), structural frames, full penetration joints, fill passes
  • Uses E7018 electrodes (low hydrogen) for mechanical strength

At the QBQ exam: Welding in 3G uphill position is generally required as it demonstrates better technical mastery. Downhill is only accepted for specific applications (pipeline per API 1104).

question: How to read welding symbols on a blueprint?
answer:
Standardized welding symbols (CSA W59 / AWS A2.4 standard) precisely indicate on a blueprint how a weld should be executed:

Symbol structure:

  • Reference line: Main horizontal line
  • Arrow: Points to the joint to be welded
  • Tail: Optional, contains additional information (process, specifications)

Elements on the reference line:

  • Above the line (other side): Weld type symbol for the side opposite the arrow
  • Below the line (arrow side): Symbol for the side the arrow points to
  • Example: Fillet weld symbol (right triangle) below the line means fillet weld on the arrow side

Additional information:

  • Size (left of symbol): Example: ¼ means ¼ in (6 mm) fillet weld
  • Length (right of symbol): Example: 3 means a 3 in (75 mm) long weld
  • Pitch (spacing): Example: 3-6 means 3 in weld every 6 in
  • Tail: Example: SMAW (process to use)

question: How to weld aluminum with TIG?
answer:
TIG (GTAW) welding on aluminum requires a special technique because of aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) which melts at 2,072°C, while aluminum melts at 660°C.

Preparation:

  • Cleaning: Aluminum must be degreased and the oxide layer removed with a stainless steel brush (dedicated brush — never used on steel)
  • Bevel: For pieces over 3 mm, a 60° bevel is recommended

Settings:

  • Current: AC (alternating current) — necessary to break the oxide in the positive half-cycle
  • Frequency: 60-120 Hz (higher frequency = more concentrated arc)
  • AC balance: 65-75% negative (penetration) / 25-35% positive (cleaning)
  • Tungsten: Thoriated (2%) or Ceriated (2%), diameter 1/16 in (1.6 mm) to 3/32 in (2.4 mm)
  • Gas: 100% Argon, flow 10-15 L/min

Filler metal:

  • ER4043 (4043 alloys) or ER5356 (5356 alloys) rods
  • Diameter: 1/16 in to 3/32 in

Tip: The aluminum weld pool is very fluid and reflective. An experienced TIG aluminum welder can achieve 50-75 mm/min on 3 mm sheet.

question: How to weld stainless steel?
answer:
Welding stainless steel (304, 316, 316L) requires special attention to avoid sensitization and distortion.

Recommended processes:

  • GTAW (TIG): For thin sheets (< 6 mm) and cosmetic work (food, pharmaceutical)
  • GMAW (MIG): For medium to thick sections, with 98% Argon + 2% CO₂ gas
  • SMAW: E308L-16 or E316L-16 electrodes for repairs

Critical points:

  • Heat input: Low (max 1.5-2 kJ/mm) — stainless distorts easily. Use a heat sink (copper) behind the weld
  • Cleaning: Dedicated stainless steel brush (never used on carbon steel — risk of iron contamination)
  • Oxidation color: A successful stainless weld is silver to light gold. Blue or gray = excessive oxidation (too much heat or insufficient gas shielding)
  • Passivation: After welding, a passivation treatment (nitric acid or pickling paste) restores the protective chromium oxide layer

Filler metal:

  • Stainless 304: ER308L rod
  • Stainless 316: ER316L rod
  • The "L" means low carbon (< 0.03%) — prevents chromium carbide precipitation

question: How to calculate the size of a fillet weld?
answer:
The fillet weld is the most common in steel structures. Here's how to calculate its size according to CSA W59:

Definitions:

  • Leg (weld size): Distance from the root to the toe of the triangle, measured on the base metal
  • Throat: Height of the largest triangle inscribed in the weld — throat = leg × 0.707 (for a 90° angle)
  • Effective throat area: Throat × weld length

Strength calculation:

  • Fillet weld shear strength: F = 0.3 × UTS × throat × length (where UTS = ultimate tensile strength of the filler metal)
  • Example: ¼ in (6 mm) fillet weld, 4 in (100 mm) long, E7018 filler metal (70,000 PSI): throat = 0.25 × 0.707 = 0.177 in. Strength = 0.3 × 70,000 × 0.177 × 4 = 14,868 lb (66 kN)

Minimum size based on piece thickness (CSA W59):

  • Thickness ≤ ¼ in (6 mm): 1/8 in (3 mm) weld
  • Thickness ¼ to ½ in (6-12 mm): 3/16 in (5 mm) weld
  • Thickness ½ to ¾ in (12-19 mm): ¼ in (6 mm) weld
  • Thickness ≥ ¾ in: 5/16 in (8 mm) weld

question: What are common weld defects and how to avoid them?
answer:
Weld defects can cause a part to be rejected at the QBQ exam. Here are the most common ones:

1. Porosity — Small holes in the weld

  • Cause: Trapped gas (moisture, draft, clogged nozzle)
  • Solution: Clean the metal, shield the arc from wind, check gas flow (12-15 L/min)

2. Undercut — Groove in the base metal along the weld toe

  • Cause: Current too high, travel speed too fast, incorrect electrode angle
  • Solution: Reduce current by 5-10%, slow down, adjust angle to 10-15°

3. Lack of fusion

  • Cause: Insufficient temperature, incorrect angle, travel speed too slow
  • Solution: Increase current, clean the joint, adjust angle

4. Slag inclusion

  • Cause: Slag from the previous pass not completely removed
  • Solution: Brushing and grinding between each pass — mandatory!

5. Longitudinal or transverse crack

  • Cause: Cooling too fast, hydrogen in the weld, high stresses
  • Solution: Low hydrogen electrodes (E7018), preheating, control of cooling rate

At the QBQ exam, lack of fusion and porosity are the most frequent causes of failure. Acceptance criteria (CSA W59): maximum porosity of 3% of the weld surface area.

question: How to set amperage and voltage in welding?
answer:
Setting amperage (current) and voltage is crucial for a quality weld. Here are the reference points for each process:

SMAW (covered electrode):

  • E6010 (3/32 in = 2.5 mm): 60-90 A
  • E6010 (1/8 in = 3.2 mm): 90-130 A
  • E7018 (1/8 in = 3.2 mm): 100-150 A (typically 120-130 A)
  • E7018 (5/32 in = 4.0 mm): 150-200 A
  • General rule: amperage = 35-40 × electrode diameter (in mm)

GMAW/MIG (solid wire):

  • 0.035 in (0.9 mm) wire: 150-200 A / 22-26 V
  • 0.045 in (1.2 mm) wire: 200-300 A / 24-30 V
  • Wire feed speed: typically 5-15 m/min

GTAW/TIG:

  • Mild steel 3 mm: 80-120 A
  • Mild steel 6 mm: 150-200 A
  • Aluminum 3 mm: 120-160 A (AC)
  • Aluminum 6 mm: 180-250 A (AC)

Visual indicators:

  • Too hot: Flat weld, too fluid, excessive spatter, excessive penetration
  • Too cold: Convex weld, lack of penetration, sticking, arc instability
  • Well set: Steady hiss, controlled weld pool, correct penetration

question: What is the API 1104 standard for pipeline welding?
answer:
The API 1104 (American Petroleum Institute) standard is the reference for welding pipelines and related facilities. It is used in Quebec for gas and oil pipelines.

Differences from CSA W59:

  • API 1104 is specific to pipelines — butt joints of steel pipes
  • Stricter acceptance criteria for certain defects
  • Requires a specific Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) qualification

Key points of API 1104:

  • Positions: Circular (5G, 6G) pipe welding
  • Root pass: Typically with E6010 (deep penetration, fast speed)
  • Fill passes: E7018 or self-shielded flux-cored wire
  • Qualification test: Guided bend test, 100% radiography of the weld, tensile tests

Radiographic rejection criteria (API 1104):

  • Porosity: no more than 3% of the projected area
  • Slag inclusions: total length < 25 mm over 150 mm of weld
  • Lack of fusion: zero tolerance
  • Crack: zero tolerance — any crack is a rejection

question: What are the welding requirements for steel structures in Quebec?
answer:
Steel structure welding in Quebec is governed by CSA W59 and the RBQ requirements.

Company requirements:

  • The company must be CSA W47.1 certified (Division 1 or 2)
  • A certified welding supervisor must be present on site
  • A quality control file must be maintained

Welder requirements:

  • QBQ certification in the required positions (min. 1G/2G for some tasks, 3G/4G for structural assemblies)
  • The welder must be qualified for the joint type and thickness being welded
  • An approved WPS (Welding Procedure Specification) is required — the welder must follow it precisely

Joint types in structures:

  • Fillet weld: Beam-column connections, gussets, connectors
  • Complete Joint Penetration (CJP) weld: For critical connections, requires ultrasonic (UT) or radiographic (RT) testing
  • Partial Joint Penetration (PJP) weld: Less demanding use

Welded structural assemblies are inspected by a CGSB Level 2 inspector. The required inspection rate (5-100%) depends on the building's seismic category.

question: How to weld angle iron correctly?
answer:
Welding angle iron (L-angle) requires a specific technique to avoid distortion and ensure structural strength.

Preparation:

  • Cleaning: Remove mill scale, rust, paint from at least 25 mm on each side of the joint
  • Edge preparation: If the angle iron is over 6 mm thick, bevel at 45° for full penetration weld

Welding technique:

  • Tack weld: Weld spots of 10-20 mm at each end and centre. For long angle (> 2 m), add tacks every 300 mm
  • Alternating welding: Weld one side then the other to distribute heat and avoid deformation. Or use skip welding sequence
  • Electrode angle: Keep the electrode at 45° between the two faces of the angle iron, with a drag angle of 5-15° (depending on position)

Distortion:

  • Angle iron tends to close or open under heat
  • Pre-clamp the angle iron in a jig or against a backup to maintain the 90° angle
  • For large production runs, pre-set the angle iron about 1-2° in the opposite direction

question: How to prepare for the QBQ practical welding exam?
answer:
The QBQ practical exam is demanding. Here are tips to maximize your chances of success:

Technical preparation:

  • Practice 100-200 hours in the exam positions (3G uphill, 4G) before showing up
  • Master E7018 electrodes: short arc technique, straight bead, clean strike
  • Train on ½ in (12 mm) plates with 30° bevel (60° total angle)
  • Learn to read your weld pool — it's more reliable than counting amps

Exam day:

  • Arrive with your own equipment: helmet, gloves, jacket, grinder, safety glasses
  • Inspect the plates before starting: check alignment, spacing (recommended root gap of 3 mm), cleanliness
  • Make quality tacks — a plate that moves during the root pass = likely failure
  • Clean every pass with a hammer and wire brush (slag = porosity on the next pass)

Most frequent failure criteria:

  • Lack of fusion at the root (unpenetrated root)
  • Undercut > 1/32 in (0.8 mm)
  • Excessive or aligned porosity
  • Crack in the weld or HAZ

question: What is lamellar tearing in welding?
answer:
Lamellar tearing is a serious defect that occurs under the weld, in the base metal, parallel to the rolled surface.

Cause:

  • Through-thickness tensile stresses (Z-direction) combined with elongated non-metallic inclusions in the rolling direction
  • Occurs especially in thick plates (> 25 mm) and T-joints (with fillet weld)
  • The through-thickness stress created by weld shrinkage exceeds the metal's strength in that direction

Prevention:

  • Material selection: Use low sulfur steels (S < 0.010%), called Z-grade steels
  • Joint design: Avoid simple T-joints. Use stiffeners, K-joints, or double bevel welds
  • Preheating: Maintain 100-150°C to reduce residual stresses
  • Welding technique: Use low heat input processes (pulsed GMAW), buttering layer with a ductile filler metal

Detection: Lamellar tearing is not visible on the surface. It is detected by ultrasonic testing (UT). This is a critical defect — the part must be rejected or repaired by grinding and rewelding.

question: Why does hydrogen cause cracking in welding?
answer:
Hydrogen-induced cracking or cold cracking is one of the most dangerous weld defects because it can appear hours or days after welding.

Mechanism:

  1. Hydrogen atoms (H) diffuse into the HAZ and weld during welding
  2. Hydrogen sources: moisture in the electrode, rust, oil, paint, dirt
  3. Hydrogen concentrates in high-stress zones (undercut, notch)
  4. When the temperature drops below 200°C, hydrogen becomes less soluble and forms H₂ molecules under pressure, creating microcracks

Aggravating factors:

  • High-strength steels (> 500 MPa yield strength)
  • Large thickness (> 25 mm)
  • Fast cooling (no preheating)
  • High residual stresses

Prevention:

  • Use low hydrogen electrodes (E7018, E8018) — stored at 120-150°C in an electrode oven
  • Dry E7018 electrodes at 300-350°C for 1 hour if packaging has been open for more than 4 hours
  • Clean the joint: remove rust, oil, paint, moisture
  • Preheating to 100-200°C to slow cooling
  • Post-heating at 200-300°C for 30 minutes allows hydrogen to diffuse out of the weld

question: How to control distortion in welding?
answer:
Distortion is the change in shape of a part caused by uneven thermal expansion and contraction during welding. Here's how to control it:

Types of distortion:

  • Longitudinal shrinkage: The weld shortens along its axis
  • Transverse shrinkage: Narrowing perpendicular to the weld bead
  • Angular distortion: The piece bends around the weld axis
  • Buckling: Deformation of thin sheet metal

Control techniques:

  • Skip welding (stitch welding): Weld in 100-200 mm segments with spacing, alternating sides. Let cool between segments
  • Presetting (pre-bending): Tilt the pieces 1-2° in the opposite direction of expected deformation
  • Clamping: Use rigid clamps to hold pieces in place. However, clamping increases residual stresses
  • Welding sequence: For a multi-pass joint, weld symmetrically (pass 1 on left, pass 2 on right)
  • Minimal heat input: Use the lowest amperage possible while maintaining fusion. Limit the number of passes

Typical shrinkage: For a butt joint in steel: about 1 mm of transverse shrinkage per 10 mm of thickness.

question: What is stud welding?
answer:
Stud welding is a process that attaches studs (threaded rods) to a metal surface. Widely used in steel construction and reinforced concrete.

Process:

  • The stud is placed in a special gun with a ceramic ferrule (arc shield)
  • An electric arc is created between the stud and the plate (current: 400-2,500 A depending on diameter)
  • After 0.1-1.5 seconds, the stud is driven into the weld pool by a spring
  • The ceramic ferrule contains the molten metal and protects the arc

Applications in Quebec:

  • Shear connectors: Studs welded onto steel beams before concrete pouring. Create composite steel-concrete action
  • Anchors: Steel plates attached to concrete (with pre-welded studs)
  • Insulation: Studs for attaching insulation and cladding to metal roofs

Standards:

  • Studs certified per CSA W59 (shear connectors)
  • Common diameters: ½ in (12.7 mm), ¾ in (19 mm), ⅞ in (22 mm)
  • Qualification test: bending to 30° (the stud must not crack)

A certified stud welder can earn $35 to $50/h on construction sites.

question: What is backgouging and why is it necessary?
answer:
Backgouging is the removal of metal from the back of a weld to expose sound metal before welding the final pass (root pass).

Why:

  • The first weld pass (root pass) often contains defects: lack of penetration, inclusions, porosity, oxidation
  • Backgouging removes these defects and exposes sound metal for the seal pass
  • Required for all Complete Joint Penetration (CJP) welds — CSA W59 standard

Methods:

  • Grinding: With a grinding wheel, for small sections. The most accessible
  • Arc gouging (carbon arc gouging — CAG): A carbon electrode with an electric arc melts the metal, then a compressed air jet blows it away. Fast and efficient method. Current: 200-600 A depending on carbon diameter
  • Chipping: With a pneumatic chipping hammer — noisy and less precise

Criteria:

  • Backgouging must be deep enough to expose metal with no visible defects (no porosity, no slag)
  • Minimum depth: 3-5 mm for a 12 mm plate
  • The gouged surface must be smooth and clean before the seal weld
  • MT (magnetic particle) or PT (dye penetrant) inspection is often required after backgouging

question: Why is 6G certification the most sought-after?
answer:
6G certification — welding a pipe inclined at 45° — is the most valued in the industry for several reasons:

Why 6G is difficult:

  • The welder must master all positions in a single weld: flat (1G), horizontal (2G), vertical (3G), and overhead (4G)
  • The fixed pipe cannot be rotated — the welder must go around the pipe, constantly changing angle and position
  • The root pass is particularly critical: it must achieve 100% penetration without sagging

Benefits of 6G certification:

  • Versatility: A 6G welder can weld in all positions on pipe and plate
  • Salary: Premium of $5 to $10/h over a 3G/4G welder
  • Portability: The certification is recognized across North America
  • Employment: Priority on major projects (pipelines, refineries, plants)

Cost of 6G certification:

  • QBQ exam: about $300 to $500 (including destructive tests)
  • Preparation training: $1,000 to $3,000 (40-80 hour course)
  • 6G certification is often combined with 6GR certification (with restriction ring — even more demanding)

question: What is FCAW (flux-cored arc welding) and when to use it?
answer:
FCAW (Flux Cored Arc Welding) is a semi-automatic process widely used in heavy construction and steel structures in Quebec.

How it works:

  • A tubular wire (flux-cored) containing flux (filler, deoxidizers, alloying elements) is automatically fed
  • The flux melts and creates a gas shield and slag that protects the weld pool
  • Two types: self-shielded — no external gas needed, ideal in wind — and gas-shielded (with CO₂ or Ar/CO₂)

Advantages:

  • High deposition rate: 3-8 kg/h (vs 1-3 kg/h for SMAW)
  • Deep penetration: Ideal for thick plates (> 12 mm) and T-joints
  • Wind resistance: Self-shielded wire can weld in moderate wind (unlike GMAW)
  • Productivity: No electrode changes — continuous feeding

Typical applications:

  • Steel structures (beams, columns, gussets)
  • Mining equipment (buckets, chassis, crushers)
  • Shipbuilding
  • Bridges and heavy structures

Cost: FCAW wire costs $5 to $10/kg (more expensive than GMAW solid wire at $3/kg, but more productive).

question: What is the difference between welding, brazing, and soldering?
answer:
The fundamental difference between welding and brazing/soldering is the melting temperature:

Welding:

  • The base metal melts — there is local fusion of the assembled pieces
  • Temperature: 1,500°C (steel) to 660°C (aluminum)
  • High mechanical strength: up to 100% of the base metal strength
  • Significant thermal expansion, possible distortion
  • Requires a certified welder for structural applications

Brazing:

  • Only the filler metal melts (temperature > 450°C), the base metal does not melt
  • Temperature: 600-900°C typically
  • Mechanical strength: 30-70% of the base metal strength
  • Minimal distortion — ideal for precision assemblies
  • Used in plumbing (copper), refrigeration, and electronics

Soldering:

  • Temperature below 450°C
  • Used in plumbing (copper pipes with solder) and electronics
  • Limited mechanical strength

In Quebec: Welding is regulated by the QBQ. Brazing in plumbing is covered by the CMMTQ. A plumber uses brazing (copper-phosphorus alloy) for type L copper pipes, with an oxyacetylene or propane torch.

question: How does oxy-fuel cutting work?
answer:
Oxy-fuel cutting is a thermal cutting process that uses a torch with fuel gas and pure oxygen. Widely used on steel structure sites in Quebec.

Principle:

  1. The metal is preheated to 900-1,000°C (steel ignition temperature) by the oxyacetylene flame
  2. A jet of pure oxygen is directed onto the heated area
  3. Oxygen reacts with iron: Fe + O₂ → FeO + heat (exothermic reaction at 1,200°C)
  4. The liquid iron oxides (slag) are blown out of the cut

Gases:

  • Acetylene (C₂H₂): Flame temperature 3,100°C. The hottest. Cost: $80 to $150/cylinder
  • Propane: Temperature 2,800°C. Cheaper ($40 to $60/cylinder), but slower preheating
  • MAP-Pro: Propane alternative, hotter. For small jobs

Capabilities:

  • Carbon steel only — does not work on stainless steel or aluminum (oxide is too adherent)
  • Thickness: up to 300 mm with a large torch
  • Cut quality: ± 2 mm precision for 25 mm plate

Safety rules:

  • Gas cylinders must be upright and chained
  • Minimum distance from heat source: 6 meters
  • Flashback arrestor mandatory on each regulator

question: Combien de questions à l'examen HVAC pour la certification CMMTQ ?
answer:
L'examen de certification HVAC (Chauffage, Ventilation et Climatisation) de la CMMTQ comporte 80 à 90 questions à choix multiples. La durée de l'examen est de 3 heures (180 minutes) et la note de passage est de 60 %.

L'examen couvre les systèmes de chauffage (fournaises, thermopompes), la ventilation (conduits, débits d'air), la climatisation (cycles frigorifiques, réfrigérants), la régulation et les thermostats, ainsi que le Code de construction du Québec applicables aux installations de chauffage et de climatisation.

Les frais d'examen sont d'environ 350 $ à 450 $. Il est recommandé de se préparer avec des simulations d'examen et des cours théoriques avant de se présenter.

question: How many questions are on the HVAC certification exam (CMMTQ)?
answer:
The HVAC (Heating, Ventilation and Air Conditioning) CMMTQ certification exam has 80 to 90 multiple-choice questions. The exam duration is 3 hours (180 minutes) and the passing grade is 60%.

The exam covers heating systems (furnaces, heat pumps), ventilation (ductwork, airflow), air conditioning (refrigeration cycles, refrigerants), controls and thermostats, as well as the Quebec Construction Code applicable to heating and cooling installations.

Exam fees are approximately $350 to $450. It is recommended to prepare with practice exams and theory courses before taking the exam.

question: Qu'est-ce que la cote SEER et quel est le minimum requis au Québec ?
answer:
Le SEER (Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio) mesure l'efficacité énergétique d'un climatiseur ou d'une thermopompe en mode refroidissement. Plus le SEER est élevé, plus l'appareil est efficace.

Au Québec, le minimum requis depuis 2023 est de 15 SEER pour les climatiseurs centraux et les thermopompes, conformément au Règlement sur l'efficacité énergétique du Canada. Les appareils les plus performants atteignent 20 à 25 SEER.

Pour le chauffage (thermopompe), on utilise le HSPF (Heating Seasonal Performance Factor). Le minimum requis est de 8,5 HSPF. Un appareil avec un SEER de 16 et un HSPF de 9,5 offre un bon équilibre coût-efficacité pour le climat québécois. Les subventions Rénoclimat peuvent atteindre 1 000 $ à 3 000 $ pour l'installation d'une thermopompe haute efficacité.

question: What is the SEER rating and what is the minimum required in Quebec?
answer:
SEER (Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio) measures the energy efficiency of an air conditioner or heat pump in cooling mode. The higher the SEER, the more efficient the unit.

In Quebec, the minimum required since 2023 is 15 SEER for central air conditioners and heat pumps, in accordance with the Canada Energy Efficiency Regulations. The most efficient units achieve 20 to 25 SEER.

For heating (heat pump), the HSPF (Heating Seasonal Performance Factor) is used. The minimum required is 8.5 HSPF. A unit with 16 SEER and 9.5 HSPF offers a good cost-effectiveness balance for the Quebec climate. Rénoclimat rebates can reach $1,000 to $3,000 for installing a high-efficiency heat pump.

question: Thermopompe ou fournaise : quel système de chauffage choisir au Québec ?
answer:
Le choix entre une thermopompe et une fournaise au Québec dépend du climat, du budget et des besoins spécifiques :

Thermopompe (pompe à chaleur) :

  • Fonctionne comme un climatiseur réversible — chauffe en hiver et refroidit en été
  • Efficacité : pour 1 kW d'électricité, elle produit 3 à 4 kW de chaleur (efficacité de 300-400 %)
  • Limite : dans les grands froids (< -25 °C), les modèles standard perdent en efficacité, mais les modèles climat froid (cold climate heat pump) fonctionnent jusqu'à -30 °C
  • Coût d'installation : 5 000 $ à 15 000 $
  • Coût d'opération : 30 à 50 % moins cher qu'une fournaise électrique

Fournaise (électrique ou au gaz) :

  • Électrique : Simple, fiable, coût d'installation bas (2 000 $ à 5 000 $). Coût d'opération : environ 1 500 $ à 2 500 $/an pour une maison moyenne
  • Au gaz : Coût d'opération environ 20-30 % moins cher que l'électrique, mais nécessite un raccordement au gaz

Recommandation : Une thermopompe climat froid combinée à une fournaise d'appoint (système bivalent) est la solution optimale pour le Québec. Elle maximise les économies d'énergie tout en assurant le confort pendant les grands froids.

question: Heat pump or furnace: which heating system to choose in Quebec?
answer:
The choice between a heat pump and a furnace in Quebec depends on climate, budget, and specific needs:

Heat pump:

  • Works as a reversible air conditioner — heats in winter and cools in summer
  • Efficiency: for 1 kW of electricity, it produces 3 to 4 kW of heat (300-400% efficiency)
  • Limitation: in extreme cold (< -25 °C), standard models lose efficiency, but cold climate heat pumps operate down to -30 °C
  • Installation cost: $5,000 to $15,000
  • Operating cost: 30 to 50% cheaper than an electric furnace

Furnace (electric or gas):

  • Electric: Simple, reliable, low installation cost ($2,000 to $5,000). Operating cost: approximately $1,500 to $2,500/year for an average home
  • Gas: Operating cost approximately 20-30% cheaper than electric, but requires a gas connection

Recommendation: A cold climate heat pump combined with a backup furnace (dual-fuel system) is the optimal solution for Quebec. It maximizes energy savings while ensuring comfort during extreme cold.

question: Quels sont les types de réfrigérants utilisés en HVAC au Québec ?
answer:
Les réfrigérants utilisés en HVAC au Québec sont réglementés par le Protocole de Montréal et les règlements canadiens sur les réfrigérants :

Réfrigérants courants :

  • R-410A (Puron) : Le plus courant depuis l'interdiction du R-22. Pression de fonctionnement élevée (350-450 PSI). Utilisé dans les climatiseurs et thermopompes modernes. Potentiel de réchauffement global (PRG/GWP) : 2 088
  • R-32 : Nouvelle génération, PRG plus bas (675), plus efficace. Gagne en popularité au Canada. Attendu comme remplacement du R-410A d'ici 2025-2030
  • R-454B : Alternative au R-410A, PRG de 466. Compatible avec les équipements existants
  • R-22 (Freon) : Interdit dans les nouveaux équipements depuis 2020. Encore utilisé en entretien (récupération et recyclage). PRG : 1 810

Règles importantes :

  • Seuls les techniciens certifiés peuvent manipuler les réfrigérants
  • La récupération des réfrigérants est obligatoire — les rejets dans l'atmosphère sont interdits
  • Les amendes pour rejet illégal peuvent atteindre 50 000 $

question: What types of refrigerants are used in HVAC in Quebec?
answer:
Refrigerants used in HVAC in Quebec are regulated by the Montreal Protocol and Canadian refrigerant regulations:

Common refrigerants:

  • R-410A (Puron): The most common since the R-22 ban. High operating pressure (350-450 PSI). Used in modern air conditioners and heat pumps. Global Warming Potential (GWP): 2,088
  • R-32: New generation, lower GWP (675), more efficient. Growing in popularity in Canada. Expected to replace R-410A by 2025-2030
  • R-454B: R-410A alternative, GWP of 466. Compatible with existing equipment
  • R-22 (Freon): Banned in new equipment since 2020. Still used in maintenance (recovery and recycling). GWP: 1,810

Important rules:

  • Only certified technicians may handle refrigerants
  • Refrigerant recovery is mandatory — release into the atmosphere is prohibited
  • Fines for illegal release can reach $50,000

question: Comment devenir technicien HVAC au Québec et quelles sont les certifications ?
answer:
Pour devenir technicien HVAC (chauffage-climatisation) au Québec, plusieurs parcours sont possibles :

Parcours principal :

  1. DEP en réfrigération (Diplôme d'études professionnelles) : 1 350 heures (environ 15 mois). Programmes offerts au CFP Léonard-de-Vinci, CFP Fierbourg, etc.
  2. ASP en réfrigération : 900 heures supplémentaires pour se spécialiser
  3. Apprentissage : 8 000 heures (4 ans) comme apprenti, supervisé par un compagnon certifié
  4. Examen CMMTQ (HVAC) : Certification en chauffage-climatisation

Certifications complémentaires :

  • Certification en manipulation des réfrigérants (OBNL/EPA) — obligatoire pour acheter et manipuler les gaz réfrigérants
  • Carte CCQ — obligatoire pour travailler sur les chantiers de construction
  • Permis RBQ — nécessaire pour opérer comme entrepreneur

Salaire : Technicien HVAC compagnon : 40 $ à 55 $/h. Salaire annuel moyen : 75 000 $ à 100 000 $. Les spécialistes en thermopompe et en réfrigération commerciale peuvent gagner davantage.

question: How to become an HVAC technician in Quebec and what certifications are needed?
answer:
To become an HVAC technician (heating and air conditioning) in Quebec, several paths are possible:

Main path:

  1. DEP in refrigeration (Professional Studies Diploma): 1,350 hours (about 15 months). Programs offered at CFP Léonard-de-Vinci, CFP Fierbourg, etc.
  2. ASP in refrigeration: 900 additional hours for specialization
  3. Apprenticeship: 8,000 hours (4 years) as an apprentice, supervised by a certified journeyman
  4. CMMTQ (HVAC) exam: Certification in heating and air conditioning

Additional certifications:

  • Refrigerant handling certification (OBNL/EPA) — mandatory to purchase and handle refrigerant gases
  • CCQ card — mandatory to work on construction sites
  • RBQ license — required to operate as a contractor

Salary: Journeyman HVAC technician: $40 to $55/h. Average annual salary: $75,000 to $100,000. Specialists in heat pumps and commercial refrigeration can earn more.

question: Combien de questions à l'examen MVL (Mécanicien véhicules lourds) ?
answer:
L'examen de certification MVL (Mécanicien de véhicules lourds) de la CCQ comporte 80 à 100 questions à choix multiples. La durée de l'examen est de 3 heures (180 minutes) et la note de passage est de 60 %.

L'examen couvre les systèmes suivants : moteurs diesel (diagnostic et réparation), transmissions (manuelles et automatiques), freins (pneumatiques et hydrauliques), systèmes hydrauliques, suspensions et directions, systèmes électriques (12 V et 24 V), et la gestion des émissions (normes EPA/Californie).

Les frais d'examen sont d'environ 250 $ à 350 $. L'examen est offert dans les centres de la CCQ à travers le Québec. Une préparation de 200 à 400 heures est recommandée pour réussir.

question: How many questions are on the MVL (Heavy Vehicle Mechanic) exam?
answer:
The MVL (Heavy Vehicle Mechanic) CCQ certification exam has 80 to 100 multiple-choice questions. The exam duration is 3 hours (180 minutes) and the passing grade is 60%.

The exam covers the following systems: diesel engines (diagnosis and repair), transmissions (manual and automatic), brakes (air and hydraulic), hydraulic systems, suspensions and steering, electrical systems (12 V and 24 V), and emissions management (EPA/California standards).

Exam fees are approximately $250 to $350. The exam is offered at CCQ centers throughout Quebec. A preparation of 200 to 400 hours is recommended to succeed.

question: Comment diagnostiquer les problèmes courants d'un moteur diesel ?
answer:
Le diagnostic des moteurs diesel sur les véhicules lourds requiert une approche systématique :

Problèmes courants et diagnostics :

  • Difficulté à démarrer : Vérifiez les bougies de préchauffage (glow plugs) — elles doivent atteindre 800-1 000 °C. Testez avec un multimètre : résistance normale de 0,5 à 2 Ω. Vérifiez aussi la pression du carburant (min 30 PSI pour les moteurs common rail)
  • Perte de puissance : Vérifiez le filtre à carburant (changement aux 15 000-30 000 km), le turbocompresseur (jeu axial max 0,5 mm), et le EGR (vanne de recirculation des gaz d'échappement) — l'encrassement du EGR est une cause fréquente
  • Fumée excessive : Fumée noire = trop de carburant (injecteurs, timing), bleue = huile (segments, joints de soupape), blanche = coolant (joint de culasse) ou gazole non brûlé
  • Bruit anormal : Cliquetis métallique peut indiquer un injecteur défectueux ou un jeu aux soupapes

L'utilisation d'un scanner diagnostic (J1939/CAN bus) est essentielle pour lire les codes d'erreur (DTC) des moteurs modernes. Les lecteurs professionnels coûtent de 1 000 $ à 5 000 $.

question: How to diagnose common diesel engine problems?
answer:
Diagnosing diesel engines on heavy vehicles requires a systematic approach:

Common problems and diagnostics:

  • Hard starting: Check the glow plugs — they must reach 800-1,000 °C. Test with a multimeter: normal resistance 0.5 to 2 Ω. Also check fuel pressure (min 30 PSI for common rail engines)
  • Power loss: Check the fuel filter (change every 15,000-30,000 km), the turbocharger (max axial play 0.5 mm), and the EGR valve — EGR clogging is a frequent cause
  • Excessive smoke: Black smoke = too much fuel (injectors, timing), blue = oil (rings, valve seals), white = coolant (head gasket) or unburned diesel
  • Abnormal noise: Metallic knocking may indicate a faulty injector or valve clearance issue

Using a diagnostic scanner (J1939/CAN bus) is essential to read error codes (DTC) on modern engines. Professional scanners cost $1,000 to $5,000.

question: Comment fonctionnent les freins pneumatiques sur un véhicule lourd ?
answer:
Les freins pneumatiques (air brakes) sont le système de freinage standard sur les camions lourds et autobus au Québec :

Principe de fonctionnement :

  • Un compresseur (entraîné par le moteur) comprime l'air à 100-125 PSI (690-860 kPa)
  • L'air comprimé est stocké dans des réservoirs (généralement 2 à 4 réservoirs d'environ 30 litres chacun)
  • Quand le conducteur appuie sur la pédale de frein, la valve de freinage envoie l'air comprimé aux chambres de frein sur chaque roue
  • La pression d'air pousse un diaphragme qui actionne la mâchoire de frein contre le tambour (ou la plaque contre le disque)

Particularité : freins à ressort (spring brakes) :

  • Les freins de stationnement sont des freins à ressort : un puissant ressort applique les freins quand il n'y a pas de pression d'air
  • Si la pression d'air descend sous 60 PSI, les freins à ressort s'appliquent automatiquement (sécurité en cas de fuite)

Entretien :

  • Vidange des réservoirs d'air : quotidienne (purge de l'eau et de l'huile accumulées)
  • Vérification du sécheur d'air (air dryer) : aux 6 mois
  • Jeu des mâchoires : réglage aux 10 000 km ou à chaque changement de plaquettes

question: How do air brakes work on a heavy vehicle?
answer:
Air brakes are the standard braking system on heavy trucks and buses in Quebec:

Operating principle:

  • A compressor (driven by the engine) compresses air to 100-125 PSI (690-860 kPa)
  • Compressed air is stored in reservoirs (usually 2 to 4 tanks of approximately 30 liters each)
  • When the driver presses the brake pedal, the brake valve sends compressed air to brake chambers on each wheel
  • Air pressure pushes a diaphragm that actuates the brake shoe against the drum (or the pad against the disc)

Feature: spring brakes:

  • Parking brakes are spring brakes: a powerful spring applies the brakes when there is no air pressure
  • If air pressure drops below 60 PSI, spring brakes apply automatically (safety in case of a leak)

Maintenance:

  • Air tank drainage: daily (drain accumulated water and oil)
  • Air dryer check: every 6 months
  • Brake shoe adjustment: every 10,000 km or at each pad change

question: Quels sont les principes de base des systèmes hydrauliques sur équipement lourd ?
answer:
Les systèmes hydrauliques sont essentiels sur les véhicules lourds (chargeurs, excavatrices, grues). Voici les principes de base :

Principe de Pascal : La pression appliquée à un fluide confiné se transmet également dans toutes les directions. Une petite force sur un petit piston peut soulever une charge lourde sur un grand piston : F2 = F1 × (A2/A1).

Composants principaux :

  • Pompe hydraulique : Transforme l'énergie mécanique en énergie hydraulique. Types : pompe à engrenages (la plus commune, pression max 3 000 PSI), pompe à pistons (haute pression, 4 000-6 000 PSI)
  • Vannes de contrôle : Dirigent le fluide vers les vérins. Les vannes proportionnelles permettent un contrôle précis des mouvements
  • Vérins hydrauliques : Transforment la pression en force linéaire. Force = Pression × Surface du piston
  • Filtre hydraulique : Protège le système des contaminants. Doit être changé aux 500-1 000 heures

Entretien :

  • Niveau d'huile hydraulique : vérifier quotidiennement
  • Température maximale de l'huile : 80 °C (au-delà, la viscosité diminue et l'usure accélère)
  • Changement d'huile : aux 2 000-4 000 heures selon le fabricant

question: What are the basic principles of hydraulic systems on heavy equipment?
answer:
Hydraulic systems are essential on heavy vehicles (loaders, excavators, cranes). Here are the basic principles:

Pascal's principle: Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. A small force on a small piston can lift a heavy load on a large piston: F2 = F1 × (A2/A1).

Main components:

  • Hydraulic pump: Converts mechanical energy into hydraulic energy. Types: gear pump (most common, max pressure 3,000 PSI), piston pump (high pressure, 4,000-6,000 PSI)
  • Control valves: Direct fluid to the cylinders. Proportional valves allow precise motion control
  • Hydraulic cylinders: Convert pressure into linear force. Force = Pressure × Piston area
  • Hydraulic filter: Protects the system from contaminants. Should be changed every 500-1,000 hours

Maintenance:

  • Hydraulic oil level: check daily
  • Maximum oil temperature: 80 °C (above that, viscosity decreases and wear accelerates)
  • Oil change: every 2,000-4,000 hours depending on the manufacturer

question: Comment devenir mécanicien de véhicules lourds au Québec et quel est le salaire ?
answer:
Devenir mécanicien de véhicules lourds au Québec prend environ 4 à 5 ans :

Parcours :

  1. DEP en mécanique de véhicules lourds routiers : 1 800 heures (environ 18 mois). Offert en plusieurs centres : CFP de Québec, CFP Mont-Joli, CFP de l'Énergie, etc.
  2. Apprentissage : 8 000 heures (4 ans) comme apprenti sous supervision d'un compagnon
  3. Examen CCQ (MVL) : Dans les centres de la CCQ

Salaire 2025 (selon la CCQ) :

  • Apprenti 1re année : environ 22 $/h
  • Apprenti 4e année : environ 35 $/h
  • Compagnon (certifié) : 44 $ à 52 $/h
  • Spécialiste (diagnostic, moteurs électroniques) : 50 $ à 65 $/h
  • Salaire annuel moyen (compagnon) : 85 000 $ à 110 000 $

Certifications complémentaires recommandées : Certification en systèmes électroniques (Cummins, Detroit, Caterpillar), en climatisation de véhicules lourds, et en soudage. Ces spécialisations peuvent augmenter le salaire de 5 $ à 10 $/h.

question: How to become a heavy vehicle mechanic in Quebec and what is the salary?
answer:
Becoming a heavy vehicle mechanic in Quebec takes approximately 4 to 5 years:

Path:

  1. DEP in heavy vehicle mechanics: 1,800 hours (about 18 months). Offered at several centers: CFP de Québec, CFP Mont-Joli, CFP de l'Énergie, etc.
  2. Apprenticeship: 8,000 hours (4 years) as an apprentice under a journeyman's supervision
  3. CCQ (MVL) exam: At CCQ centers

Salary 2025 (per CCQ):

  • 1st year apprentice: approximately $22/h
  • 4th year apprentice: approximately $35/h
  • Journeyman (certified): $44 to $52/h
  • Specialist (diagnostics, electronic engines): $50 to $65/h
  • Average annual salary (journeyman): $85,000 to $110,000

Recommended additional certifications: Certification in electronic systems (Cummins, Detroit, Caterpillar), heavy vehicle air conditioning, and welding. These specializations can increase salary by $5 to $10/h.

question: Combien de questions à l'examen de technicien en sécurité incendie ?
answer:
L'examen de certification pour technicien en sécurité incendie (RBQ) comporte 70 à 90 questions à choix multiples. La durée de l'examen est de 3 heures et la note de passage est de 60 %.

L'examen couvre : la norme NFPA 72 (systèmes d'alarme incendie et de détection), la norme NFPA 13 (gicleurs), la norme NFPA 20 (pompes à incendie), le Code de construction du Québec, Chapitre I — Bâtiment, et les systèmes de communication d'urgence.

Les frais d'examen sont d'environ 300 $ à 400 $. Une préparation de 200 à 400 heures d'étude est recommandée, incluant la maîtrise des normes NFPA applicables.

question: How many questions are on the fire safety technician exam?
answer:
The certification exam for fire safety technician (RBQ) has 70 to 90 multiple-choice questions. The exam duration is 3 hours and the passing grade is 60%.

The exam covers: NFPA 72 standard (fire alarm and detection systems), NFPA 13 (sprinklers), NFPA 20 (fire pumps), the Quebec Construction Code, Chapter I — Building, and emergency communication systems.

Exam fees are approximately $300 to $400. A preparation of 200 to 400 hours of study is recommended, including mastery of applicable NFPA standards.

question: Quels sont les types de systèmes d'alarme incendie et de détection ?
answer:
Les systèmes d'alarme incendie sont classés selon la norme NFPA 72 et le Code de construction du Québec :

Types de systèmes :

  • Système conventionnel : Zones définies (ex : 8 zones). Quand un détecteur se déclenche, la zone est identifiée mais pas l'appareil exact. Utilisé dans les petits bâtiments. Coût : 2 000 $ à 5 000 $
  • Système adressable : Chaque détecteur a une adresse unique. Le panneau indique exactement quel détecteur s'est déclenché. Obligatoire dans les bâtiments de plus de 3 étages ou 4 000 m². Coût : 5 000 $ à 20 000 $

Types de détecteurs :

  • Détecteur de fumée ionisation : Détecte les particules invisibles (feux à combustion rapide). Sensible aux fausses alarmes en cuisine
  • Détecteur de fumée photodectrique : Détecte les particules visibles (feux couvants). Meilleur pour les corridors et chambres
  • Détecteur de chaleur : Se déclenche à une température fixe (57 °C ou 93 °C) ou à une augmentation rapide de la température (rate-of-rise)
  • Détecteur de monoxyde de carbone (CO) : Obligatoire dans les bâtiments avec stationnement intérieur ou appareils au gaz

Les détecteurs doivent être testés annuellement et remplacés tous les 10 ans.

question: What are the types of fire alarm and detection systems?
answer:
Fire alarm systems are classified according to NFPA 72 and the Quebec Construction Code:

System types:

  • Conventional system: Defined zones (e.g., 8 zones). When a detector activates, the zone is identified but not the exact device. Used in small buildings. Cost: $2,000 to $5,000
  • Addressable system: Each detector has a unique address. The panel indicates exactly which detector activated. Mandatory in buildings over 3 stories or 4,000 m². Cost: $5,000 to $20,000

Detector types:

  • Ionization smoke detector: Detects invisible particles (fast-flaming fires). Prone to false alarms in kitchens
  • Photoelectric smoke detector: Detects visible particles (smoldering fires). Best for corridors and bedrooms
  • Heat detector: Activates at a fixed temperature (57 °C or 93 °C) or at a rapid temperature rise (rate-of-rise)
  • Carbon monoxide (CO) detector: Mandatory in buildings with indoor parking or gas appliances

Detectors must be tested annually and replaced every 10 years.

question: Quelles sont les principales normes NFPA pour la sécurité incendie au Québec ?
answer:
Les normes NFPA (National Fire Protection Association) sont la référence en sécurité incendie au Québec :

Normes principales :

  • NFPA 72 — Code national de l'alarme incendie : Couvre la conception, l'installation, la mise à l'essai et l'entretien des systèmes d'alarme incendie. Exige une inspection visuelle tous les 6 mois et un test complet annuel
  • NFPA 13 — Installation des systèmes de gicleurs : Norme complète pour les systèmes automatiques d'extinction. Classification des risques : Léger, Ordinaire (groupe 1 et 2), Extra (groupe 1 et 2)
  • NFPA 72, annexe B — Signaux d'alarme : Exigences sur les signaux audibles (minimum 75 dBA dans les chambres) et visibles (stroboscopes)
  • NFPA 20 — Pompes à incendie : Installation et entretien des pompes à incendie (diesel, électrique ou à vapeur)
  • NFPA 10 — Extincteurs portatifs : Types, installation, inspection annuelle et recharge aux 6 ans (extincteurs à pression) ou 12 ans (CO₂)

Le Code de construction du Québec adopte ces normes avec des modifications propres au Québec. Les inspecteurs RBQ vérifient la conformité.

question: What are the main NFPA standards for fire safety in Quebec?
answer:
NFPA (National Fire Protection Association) standards are the reference for fire safety in Quebec:

Main standards:

  • NFPA 72 — National Fire Alarm Code: Covers the design, installation, testing, and maintenance of fire alarm systems. Requires visual inspection every 6 months and a full annual test
  • NFPA 13 — Sprinkler System Installation: Complete standard for automatic extinguishing systems. Hazard classification: Light, Ordinary (group 1 and 2), Extra (group 1 and 2)
  • NFPA 72, Annex B — Alarm Signals: Requirements for audible signals (minimum 75 dBA in bedrooms) and visible signals (strobes)
  • NFPA 20 — Fire Pumps: Installation and maintenance of fire pumps (diesel, electric, or steam)
  • NFPA 10 — Portable Fire Extinguishers: Types, installation, annual inspection, and recharge every 6 years (pressure extinguishers) or 12 years (CO₂)

The Quebec Construction Code adopts these standards with Quebec-specific modifications. RBQ inspectors verify compliance.

question: Quels sont les différents types d'extincteurs et leurs usages ?
answer:
Les extincteurs portatifs sont classés selon la norme NFPA 10 par classe de feu :

Classes de feu :

  • Classe A : Feux de matériaux solides (bois, papier, tissu). Extincteurs à eau, à mousse, ou ABC à poudre
